coredata.dc:title: Strongly coordinated synthesis of high-loading Ta2O5-supported Ru-Fe-Ni single-atom electrocatalyst with enhanced catalytic performance for urea synthesis via multifunctional graphene quantum dot integration

coredata.dc:description: Industrial application of oxide-supported single atom catalysts remains many challenges, including poor economic feasibility, insufficient catalytic activity, limited electrical conductivity, and lack of functional diversity. The study reports a coordination-driven synthesis strategy for fabricating tantalum pentoxide-supported ruthenium‑iron‑nickel single atom electrocatalyst (Ru-Fe-Ni/Ta2O5@DHB-GQD) through metal ion coordination with aspartic acid and histidine-functionalized boron-doped graphene quantum dot (DHB-GQD), followed by controlled annealing. The resulting Ru-Fe-Ni/ Ta2O5@DHB-GQD offers atomic dispersion of Ru, Fe, and Ni on Ta2O5 with high metal loading of 5.6 wt%. Ta2O5 substrate features unique shear-structure with interconnected tunnels, enabling catalytic reactions to proceed simultaneously on the nanocrystal surfaces and within tunnel walls. Integration of DHB-GQD with Ru, Fe and Ni optimizes tunnel structure, reduces bandgap by 0.8 eV, and establishes efficient electron transfer pathways. Compared to pristine Ta2O5, engineered catalyst exhibits an enhancement in electrochemical active surface area (>4-fold), electrical conductivity (>53-fold), and K+ diffusion efficiency (>1564-fold). Experimental analyses coupled with density functional theory calculation demonstrates that trimetallic synergy of Ru, Fe and Ni significantly improves the catalytic activity and selectivity for reduction of NO3 − and CO2 into urea. The Ru-Fe-Ni/ Ta2O5@DHB-GQD electrocatalyst achieves urea yield of 73.86 mmol g−1 h−1 with Faradaic efficiency of 48.5 %, surpassing previously reported counterparts.

head.ce:title: Strongly coordinated synthesis of high-loading Ta2O5-supported Ru-Fe-Ni single-atom electrocatalyst with enhanced catalytic performance for urea synthesis via multifunctional graphene quantum dot integration

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Industrial application of oxide-supported single atom catalysts remains many challenges, including poor economic feasibility, insufficient catalytic activity, limited electrical conductivity, and lack of functional diversity. The study reports a coordination-driven synthesis strategy for fabricating tantalum pentoxide-supported ruthenium‑iron‑nickel single atom electrocatalyst (Ru-Fe-Ni/Ta2O5@DHB-GQD) through metal ion coordination with aspartic acid and histidine-functionalized boron-doped graphene quantum dot (DHB-GQD), followed by controlled annealing. The resulting Ru-Fe-Ni/ Ta2O5@DHB-GQD offers atomic dispersion of Ru, Fe, and Ni on Ta2O5 with high metal loading of 5.6 wt%. Ta2O5 substrate features unique shear-structure with interconnected tunnels, enabling catalytic reactions to proceed simultaneously on the nanocrystal surfaces and within tunnel walls. Integration of DHB-GQD with Ru, Fe and Ni optimizes tunnel structure, reduces bandgap by 0.8 eV, and establishes efficient electron transfer pathways. Compared to pristine Ta2O5, engineered catalyst exhibits an enhancement in electrochemical active surface area (>4-fold), electrical conductivity (>53-fold), and K+ diffusion efficiency (>1564-fold). Experimental analyses coupled with density functional theory calculation demonstrates that trimetallic synergy of Ru, Fe and Ni significantly improves the catalytic activity and selectivity for reduction of NO3 − and CO2 into urea. The Ru-Fe-Ni/ Ta2O5@DHB-GQD electrocatalyst achieves urea yield of 73.86 mmol g−1 h−1 with Faradaic efficiency of 48.5 %, surpassing previously reported counterparts.

ce:section.ce:para: Single atom catalysts (SACs) have emerged as a transformative class of materials with extensive potential applications in catalysis, energy storage and conversion, sensing, biomedicine, pollutant degradation, and organic synthesis, owing to their exceptional atom utilization efficiency, intrinsic reactivity, and selectivity [1–4]. Nevertheless, a critical challenge hindering their widespread adoption is the propensity of isolated metal atoms to sinter into thermodynamically stable nanoclusters under operational conditions, driven by their inherently high surface energy. To address this issue, the judicious selection of a robust solid support is paramount, as it not only anchors and stabilizes the metal species but also modulates the coordination environment and electronic properties of the active sites, thereby enhancing catalytic performance [5].

The current supports for SACs are broadly classified into carbon-based and non‑carbon-based materials. Carbonaceous supports such as graphene, graphdiyne, and MXene immobilize metal atoms primarily through defects, heteroatom doping, or functional groups [6]. While these materials offer advantages such as high electrical conductivity and large specific surface areas, their practical utility is constrained by inherently low metal loadings (<1 wt%) due to limited anchoring sites. Moreover, weak metal-support interactions often lead to metal detachment at elevated temperatures, further compromising catalytic activity and thermal stability [7]. In contrast, non‑carbon supports—particularly metal oxides—have garnered significant attention owing to their tailorable compositions, structural versatility, and strong metal-support interactions. These features enable the design of high-performance SACs with enhanced metal loading, selectivity, and stability [8]. Metal oxide supports, for instance, provide abundant defect sites, functional groups (e.g., surface hydroxyls), and tunable redox properties, making them ideal for applications ranging from industrial catalysis to organic functionalization [9]. Despite these merits, stabilizing single metal atoms on oxide supports remains a formidable challenge.

Numerous methodologies have been developed to fabricate the oxide-supported SACs with high metal loading, including physical approaches such as atomic layer deposition (ALD), high-temperature dispersion, and mechanochemical ball milling, as well as chemical routes like coprecipitation and impregnation [10]. ALD employs sequential self-limiting surface reactions to deposit metal atoms with atomic precision; however, its scalability is hindered by precursor costs, prolonged processing times, and the tendency for metal clustering during deposition [11]. High-temperature dispersion involves annealing preformed metal nanoparticles to vaporize and redistribute metal atoms onto oxide supports. For example, a “wrap-bake-peel” strategy was reported to anchor single Rh atoms on CeO2 (210) facets, achieving enhanced CO oxidation activity [12]. However, excessive temperatures may induce support sintering, degrading structural integrity. Mechanochemical ball milling, while scalable, suffers from energy inefficiency and uncontrolled metal aggregation [13].

Chemical routes, particularly coprecipitation and impregnation, offer greater practicality. In coprecipitation, simultaneous precipitation of metal hydroxides facilitates uniform metal dispersion, yet stringent requirements for precursor solubility and precipitation kinetics often lead to inhomogeneous distributions [14]. Impregnation, conversely, achieves near-complete atomic utilization by adsorbing metal precursors onto oxide surfaces followed by thermal activation. Recent innovations, such as electrostatic adsorption, photochemical deposition, and ligand-assisted stabilization, have further optimized this method [15–22]. Despite these advances, oxide-supported SACs face persistent challenges: (1) limited scalability due to specialized equipment requirements; (2) suboptimal catalytic activity compared to conventional catalysts, driven by low metal loadings; (3) poor electrical conductivity inherent to semiconducting oxides; and (4) insufficient functional diversity for complex catalytic processes.

In this study, we present a scalable synthesis of a high-loading, three-dimensional Ta2O5-supported Ru-Fe-Ni single-atom catalyst (denoted as Ru-Fe-Ni/Ta2O5@DHB-GQD) via aspartic acid and histidine-functionalized boron-doped graphene quantum dots (DHB-GQD) as coordination mediators. The Ta2O5 precursor synergistically isolates Ru, Fe, and Ni atoms, achieving an unprecedented metal loading of 5.6 wt%. The resulting hierarchical architecture features cube-like Ta2O5 nanocrystals with interconnected tunnels, enabling catalytic reactions on both surface and subsurface sites. The integration of DHB-GQD enhances electrochemical surface area, electrical conductivity, ion diffusion kinetics, and functional diversity. When employed as a three-dimensional electrocatalyst for urea synthesis, Ru-Fe-Ni/Ta2O5@DHB-GQD delivers a high urea yield of 73.86 mmol g−1 h−1 with a Faradaic efficiency of 48.5 %, surpassing state-of-the-art catalysts. This work provides a paradigm for designing high-performance SACs with industrial relevance.

DHB-GQD was synthesized by heating a mixture of citric acid (6.0 g), aspartic acid (2.1 g), histidine (4.0 g), and boric acid (0.7 g) at 180 °C for 4 h under ambient atmosphere [23]. The resulting DHB-GQD (600 mg) was dispersed in 10 mL of ultrapure water. Subsequently, freshly prepared tantalic acid (Ta(OH)5, 600 mg) [24], ruthenium trichloride hydrate (RuCl3·3H2O, 47 mg), ferric chloride (FeCl3, 43 mg), nickel chloride hexahydrate (NiCl2·6H₂O, 60 mg), and 5 mL of 30 % hydrogen peroxide solution were added sequentially. The mixture was mechanically stirred at 60 °C (200 rpm) until a homogeneous transparent solution formed. The product was vacuum-dried at 50 °C for 12 h and annealed under N₂ atmosphere at 900 °C for 2 h (heating rate: 1.5 °C min−1) to yield the final Ru-Fe-Ni/Ta2O5@DHB-GQD catalyst.

For comparative studies, control samples were synthesized using identical procedures with the following modifications: Ta2O5: Excluded DHB-GQD, RuCl3, FeCl3, and NiCl2. Ta2O5@DHB-GQD: Excluded RuCl3, FeCl3, and NiCl2. Ru/Ta2O5@DHB-GQD, Fe/Ta2O5@DHB-GQD, and Ni/Ta2O5@ DHB-GQD: Omitted FeCl3/NiCl2, RuCl3/NiCl2, and RuCl3/FeCl3, respectively. Ru-Fe/ Ta2O5@DHB-GQD, Ru-Ni/ Ta2O5@DHB-GQD, and Fe-Ni/Ta2O5@DHB-GQD: Omitted NiCl2, FeCl3, and RuCl3, respectively.

A conventional H-cell electrochemical reactor, partitioned by a Nafion 117 membrane, was employed for urea synthesis. The working electrode was prepared by dispersing 2–5 mg of Ru-Fe-Ni/Ta2O5@DHB-GQD in a solution of 950 μL ethanol and 50 μL 5.0 wt% Nafion, followed by 30 min sonication. A 20 μL aliquot of the suspension was drop-cast onto carbon paper (1 cm × 1 cm) and dried under vacuum at 80 °C for 24 h. The three-electrode system comprised the as-prepared working electrode, a Pt foil counter electrode (1 cm × 1 cm), and an Ag/AgCl reference electrode.

The electrocatalytic reaction was conducted in a 0.1 M KNO₃ electrolyte saturated with CO₂ (flow rate: 30 mL min−1) using a CHI830D electrochemical workstation. Prior to testing, the electrolyte was purged with Ar for 30 min to eliminate dissolved oxygen. Potentials measured versus Ag/AgCl were converted to the reversible hydrogen electrode (RHE) scale using the equation: ERHE = EAg/AgCl + 0.197 + 0.0592 × pH.

Density functional theory (DFT) simulations were performed using the Materials Studio package. The Ta₂O₅ (001) surface (Materials Project ID: mp-1,405,465) was modeled to represent the catalytic interface. For Ru-Fe-Ni/Ta2O5@DHB-GQD, the surface was modified with graphene-like hexagonal rings and selective substitution of Ta atoms with Ru, Fe, and Ni. A vacuum layer of 15 Å along the z-axis minimized periodic interactions.

The Perdew–Burke–Ernzerhof (PBE) functional under generalized gradient approximation (GGA) with norm-conserving pseudopotentials was used for electron–electron interactions. Geometric optimizations employed a double numerical basis set and a Monkhorst–Pack k-point grid (1 × 2 × 1). Convergence criteria included an SCF tolerance of 1.0 × 10−5 Ha, energy thresholds of 1.0 × 10−5 Ha, and maximum forces of 0.004 Ha Å−1.

Transition-state energy barriers were computed via the climbing image nudged elastic band (CI-NEB) method. Gibbs free energy changes (ΔG) were derived using the computational hydrogen electrode (CHE) model, where: ΔG = ΔE + ΔZPE - T\ΔS, with E, ZPE, T, and S denoting reaction energy, zero-point energy, temperature (298.15 K), and entropy, respectively. Adsorption energies (ΔE) were calculated as: ΔEads = Ecat/adsorbate - Ecat - Eadsorbate. Density functional theory (DFT) simulations were performed using the Materials Studio package. The Ta2O5 (001) surface (Materials Project ID: mp-1,405,465) was modeled to represent the catalytic interface. For Ru-Fe-Ni/Ta2O5@DHB-GQD, the surface was modified with graphene-like hexagonal rings and selective substitution of Ta atoms with Ru, Fe, and Ni. A vacuum layer of 15 Å along the z-axis minimized periodic interactions.
Supplementary characterization data (e.g., TEM, XPS, XRD) and extended catalytic performance metrics are provided in the Electronic Supplementary Information.

Fig. 1 outlines the design and synthesis protocols for DHB-GQD and Ru-Fe-Ni/Ta2O5@DHB-GQD. The preparation of DHB-GQD begins with the thermal treatment (180 °C, 4 h) of a mixture containing citric acid, aspartic acid, histidine, and H₃BO₃. In this process, citric acid acts as a carbon precursor to generate graphene quantum dots (GQDs) [23], while aspartic acid and histidine functionalize the edges of the graphene sheets with carboxyl, imidazolyl, and imide groups. These functional groups enable robust coordination with transition metal ions, forming stable complexes. Concurrently, H3BO3 introduces boron atoms into the graphene lattice, enhancing its semiconducting properties. Comprehensive characterization via transmission electron microscopy (TEM), atomic force microscopy (AFM), Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), and fluorescence spectroscopy (see Fig. S1) confirmed that DHB-GQD comprises graphene sheets with an average lateral size of 4.62 nm and abundant surface functionalities (-OH, -NH-, -COOH, imidazolyl).

The synthesized DHB-GQD was subsequently employed as a multifunctional chelating agent, morphology modulator, and semiconductor scaffold for the fabrication of Ru-Fe-Ni/Ta2O5@DHB-GQD. Initially, Ta5+, Ru3+, Fe3+, and Ni2+ ions were coordinated with DHB-GQD in aqueous solution, yielding a transparent, water-soluble metal-DHB-GQD complex (Fig. S2). The strong chelation capacity of DHB-GQD facilitated rapid dissolution of turbid tantalum acid precursors and ensured homogeneous dispersion of metal ions within the reaction matrix. Crucially, coordination bonds between the functional groups of DHB-GQD and metal ions prevented segregation during vacuum drying, maintaining uniform spatial distribution of Ta, Ru, Fe, and Ni species in both solution and solid states. This homogeneity suppressed aggregation of active metals, thereby maximizing metal loading efficiency.

Thermal annealing of metal-DHB-GQD complex under N₂ atmosphere induced structural evolution into Ru-Fe-Ni/Ta2O5@DHB-GQD. During this step, Ta5+ species were converted into Ta2O₅ nanocrystals, while Ru3+, Fe3+, and Ni2+ ions occupied substitutional sites within the Ta2O₅ lattice, forming atomically dispersed single-metal sites. Concurrently, DHB-GQD underwent partial reduction to graphene sheets, which adhered to Ta2O5 nanocrystals. Due to excellent conductivity, the graphene coating accelerates the electron transfer between Ta2O5 and graphene. The synthesis strategy—comprising solution coordination, vacuum drying, and thermal annealing—aligns with scalable industrial protocols, offering a cost-effective route for high-throughput production of oxide-supported SACs) with superior conductivity and metal loading.

The incorporation of Ru, Fe, and Ni into Ta2O5 confers dual advantages. First, the distinct ionic radii of Ru3+ (0.68 Å), Fe3+ (0.645 Å), and Ni2+ (0.56 Å) relative to Ta5+ (0.64 Å) [25,26] induce the lattice strain, optimizing the shear structure of Ta2O5 to create three-dimensional interconnected tunnels (Fig. 1B). These tunnels facilitate rapid electrolyte diffusion during the electrocatalytic urea synthesis, enabling simultaneous catalytic activity on Ta2O5 surfaces and within internal tunnels. Second, the synergistic interplay among Ru, Fe, and Ni enhances functional diversity: Ru promotes CN coupling, Fe stabilizes reaction intermediates, and Ni modulates electron transfer kinetics [27]. This multi-metallic design achieves a balance between high activity and selectivity, addressing the mechanistic complexity of urea electrosynthesis.

The structure of Ru-Fe-Ni/Ta2O5@DHB-GQD was systematically investigated using scanning electron microscopy (SEM), transmission electron microscopy (TEM), high-resolution TEM (HRTEM), high-angle annular dark-field scanning TEM (HAADF-STEM), and elemental mapping. As shown in Fig. 2A, B, and D, the Ta2O5 nanocrystals adopt a well-defined cubic morphology with sharp edges and a relatively wide range of sizes from a few nanometers to 200 nm. To elucidate the role of DHB-GQD in morphology regulation, control experiments were conducted by synthesizing Ru-Fe-Ni/Ta2O5@DHB-GQD with varying DHB-GQD concentrations. In the absence of DHB-GQD, Ta2O5 particles exhibited irregular agglomerates exceeding several hundred nanometers in size (see Fig. S3). This aggregation originates from the hydrolysis of Ta5+ into sparingly soluble Ta(OH)5 in aqueous media, which precipitates and coalesces into large particles.

Introducing DHB-GQD dramatically altered the nucleation and growth kinetics of Ta2O5. At low DHB-GQD concentrations (∼10 nm spherical particles with nascent faceting), whereas higher concentrations induced a progressive transition to rectangular, cubic, and hexahedral morphologies. This tunability underscores DHB-GQD's dual role as a chelating agent and morphology-directing template, though the precise mechanistic interplay warrants further investigation. HRTEM imaging (Fig. 2C) revealed a lattice spacing of 0.56 nm, corresponding to the (402) plane of shear-structured Ta2O5, consistent with its bronze-type crystallographic arrangement.

Elemental mapping (Fig. 2E–I) confirmed the spatial distribution of Ta and O (localized on Ta2O5 nanocubes) versus C, B, and N (homogeneously distributed, indicative of graphene sheets derived from DHB-GQD decomposition). Inductively coupled plasma optical emission spectrometry (ICP-OES) was used for detection of Fe, Ni and Ru in Ru-Fe-Ni/Ta2O5@DHB-GQD. The mass ratios are 1.74 wt% for Fe, 1.72 wt% for Ni, and 2.12 wt% for Ru, and total active metal loading is about 5.6 wt%. Notably, HAADF-STEM (Fig. 2D) and energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) confirmed the absence of metal clusters, validating the atomic dispersion of Ru, Fe, and Ni within the Ta2O5 lattice.

The atomic-scale dispersion of Ru, Fe, and Ni on the Ta2O5 support was elucidated via high-angle annular dark-field scanning transmission electron microscopy (HAADF-STEM) and atomically resolved elemental mapping (Fig. 3 ). HAADF-STEM imaging (Fig. 3A) and the corresponding inverse fast Fourier transform (IFFT) pattern (Fig. 3B) revealed discrete bright and dark dots, corresponding to atomic columns. The bright dots exhibited an average diameter of 0.15 ± 0.07 nm, consistent with the atomic radius of Ta. Owing to its highest atomic number, Ta atoms displayed the greatest intensity contrast in HAADF-STEM imaging, while the subtle brightness variations between Ta, Ru, Fe, and Ni necessitated complementary elemental mapping for unambiguous identification.

Line-scan profiles derived from IFFT analysis of selected regions (Lines 1 and 2 in Fig. 3B) resolved individual atomic columns. For Line 1 (Fig. 3C), sequential intensity peaks were assigned to Ta, Ta, Ta, Ru, Fe, and Ni (left to right), whereas line 2 (Fig. 3D) revealed Ru, Fe, and Ni atoms in a non-clustered arrangement. Elemental mapping further corroborated these findings: Ta signals (Fig. 3E) exhibited dense, homogeneous distribution across the Ta2O5 matrix, while Ru, Fe, and Ni (Figs. 3F–H) showed sparse, isolated atomic sites. The nearest-neighbor distances between active metal atoms—1.0 nm for Ru, 0.7 nm for Fe, and 0.8 nm for Ni—far exceed typical metal-metal bond lengths (e.g., Fe–Fe: ∼0.25 nm, Ni–Ni: ∼0.25 nm, Ru–Ru: ∼0.27 nm), confirming their atomic-level dispersion.

Atomic strain mapping (Fig. 3I) revealed localized lattice distortions (<1 nm range) within the Ta2O5 framework, attributed to the substitutional incorporation of Ru, Fe, and Ni atoms. These heteroatoms induce short-range stress fields, evidenced by undulating strain patterns, which reflect lattice rearrangements to accommodate atomic mismatch (Ru3+: 0.68 Å, Fe3+: 0.645 Å, Ni2+: 0.56 Å vs. Ta5+: 0.64 Å [25,26]). Such strain modulation is critical for stabilizing single-atom sites while preserving structural integrity. Collectively, these results validate the successful synthesis of Ru-Fe-Ni/Ta2O5@DHB-GQD as a high-density single-atom catalyst with minimal metal aggregation.

The electronic and coordination structure of Ru, Fe, and Ni within the Ru-Fe-Ni/Ta2O5@DHB-GQD catalyst were systematically probed using X-ray absorption near-edge spectroscopy (XANES) and extended X-ray absorption fine structure (EXAFS) (Fig. 4 ). The Fe K-edge XANES spectrum (Fig. 4A) exhibits an absorption edge energy and rising-edge intensity intermediate between Fe foil (metallic Fe0) and Fe2O3 (Fe3+), closely aligning with Fe3O4 (mixed Fe2+/Fe3+), confirming the coexistence of Fe2+ and Fe3+ oxidation states. The R-space EXAFS spectrum (Fig. 4D) reveals two dominant peaks at 1.58 Å and 3.29 Å, corresponding to FeO and Fe–O–Ta coordination shells, respectively. The absence of FeFe (2.21 Å) or Fe–O–Fe (2.87 Å) scattering paths [28] corroborates the atomic dispersion of Fe within the Ta2O5 lattice. For Ni (Fig. 4B), the Ni K-edge XANES absorption edge aligns with NiO (Ni2+), positioned between Ni foil (Ni0) and NiO, confirming a predominant Ni2+ state. EXAFS fitting (Fig. 4E) identifies NiO (1.71 Å) and Ni–O–Ta (3.29 Å) bonds, with no evidence of NiNi (2.48 Å) or Ni–O–Ni (2.09 Å) interactions [29], further validating single-atom Ni incorporation. The Ru K-edge XANES spectrum (Fig. 4C) displays an edge energy near RuO2 (Ru4+), intermediate between Ru foil (Ru0) and RuO2, indicative of mixed Ru3+/Ru4+ states. EXAFS analysis (Fig. 4F) resolves RuO (1.58 Å) and Ru–O–Ta (3.32 Å) coordination, with no RuRu (2.67 Å) contributions [30], confirming Ru exists as isolated sites. Quantitative EXAFS fitting (Table S1) yields coordination numbers of 2.2 (FeO), 4.1 (Fe–O–Ta), 2.3 (NiO), 2.7 (Ni–O–Ta), 2.5 (RuO), and 1.9 (Ru–O–Ta), consistent with metal atoms occupying Ta5+ substitutional sites via oxygen-mediated bonds. These results unequivocally demonstrate that Ru, Fe, and Ni are atomically dispersed and chemically anchored within the Ta2O5 matrix.

Chemical environment and surface of Ru-Fe-Ni/Ta2O5@DHB-GQD were comprehensively characterized using Raman spectroscopy, Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR), and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) (Fig. 5 ). The Raman spectrum (Fig. 5A) displays characteristic D (1323.8 cm−1) and G (1607.2 cm−1) bands of graphene, with an ID/IG ratio of 1.34, indicative of highly dispersed graphene sheets with structural defects [31]. Additional peaks at 636.2, 253.3, and 73.7 cm−1 correspond to TaO stretching vibrations, confirming the integration of Ta2O5 within the composite [32].

FTIR analysis (Fig. 5B) reveals functional group signatures: a broad band at ∼3410 cm−1 (N–H/O–H stretching), a peak at 2915 cm−1 (CH stretching), and bands at 1616 cm−1 (C=O/C=C), 1067 cm−1 (CO), and 636 cm−1 (TaO). These features corroborate the presence of DHB-GQD-derived aromatic carbon frameworks and Ta2O5 coordination.

The EPR spectrum of pristine Ta2O5 exhibits negligible signal intensity, suggesting minimal low-valent Ta species (Fig. 5C). In contrast, Ta2O5@DHB-GQD shows a distinct signal at g = 2.002, attributed to Ta4+ and oxygen vacancies induced by DHB-GQD-mediated reduction [33–35]. Remarkably, Ru-Fe-Ni/Ta2O5@DHB-GQD demonstrates intensified EPR signals, implying synergistic enhancement of oxygen vacancies via Ru/Fe/Ni incorporation.

The survey XPS spectrum (Fig. 5D) confirms the presence of Ta, C, N, O, and B, while Ru/Fe/Ni signals are obscured due to low metal loading and graphene shielding. High-resolution spectra reveal: Ta4f (Fig. 5E): Doublets at 24.96/26.39 eV (Ta4+4f7/2/Ta4+4f5/2) and 26.96/28.33 eV (Ta5+), validating mixed oxidation states [36]. O1s (Fig. 5F): Peaks at 530.5 eV (TaO) and 532.1 eV (C–O–C/O–C=O), reflecting oxide and graphene‑oxygen interactions. C1s (Fig. 5G): Contributions from CC (284.8 eV), CN (285.1 eV), CO (286.5 eV), and CO (289.6 eV), consistent with DHB-GQD's functionalized structure. N1s (Fig. 5H): Peaks at 399.9 eV (pyrrolic N), 403.6 eV (amine N), and 405.1 eV (graphitic N), confirming nitrogen doping. B1s (Fig. 5I): Components at 190.9 eV (BC2), 191.7 eV (BC2O), and 192.4 eV (BCO2), evidencing boron integration [25].

The results demonstrate that the composite's functionalized graphene and oxygen vacancies enhance electrolyte-catalyst affinity, facilitating charge transfer and boosting urea electrosynthesis efficiency. Oxygen vacancies and heteroatom doping (B/N) collectively narrow the Ta2O5 bandgap, elevating electronic conductivity and catalytic activity.

Crystallographic features and electrochemical surface activity of Ru-Fe-Ni/Ta2O5@DHB-GQD were investigated via X-ray diffraction (XRD) and electrochemical surface area (ECSA) measurements (Fig. 6 ). The XRD pattern ( Fig. 6 A) exhibits distinct diffraction peaks at 14.6°, 16.7°, 18.2°, 23.1°, 26.9°, 29.4°, 29.9°, 31.9°, 32.6°, 35.1°, 36.7°, 38.1°, 44.2°, 45.4°, 47.4°, 51.1°, 52.7°, 53.8°, and 59.9°, corresponding to the (001), (401), (900), (010), (111), (302), (402), (1401), (911), (1111), (1102), (1311), (303), (2200), (2300), (1020), (921), (2600), and (2212) planes of shear-structured Ta2O5 (JCPDS No. 25–0922). [37] These reflections confirm the formation of phase-pure Ta2O5 nanocrystals with a bronze-type lattice. Structural modeling (Figs. 6B–D) reveals a framework of corner-sharing TaO6 octahedra arranged into 3 × 3 × ∞ ReO₃-type blocks, interspersed with RuO₆/FeO6/NiO6 octahedra. This configuration generates three-dimensional interconnected tunnels (lattice parameters: a = 43.99 Å, b = 3.89 Å, c = 6.21 Å), which facilitate rapid electrolyte diffusion and enable catalytic activity across both surface and subsurface regions [38].

Electrochemical Surface Area (ECSA) analysis was performed in 0.1 M KNO₃ using cyclic voltammetry (CV) at varying scan rates (Figs. 6E, S4). The Ru-Fe-Ni/Ta2O5@DHB-GQD electrode exhibited the highest current density, surpassing Ta2O5, Ta2O5@DHB-GQD, and TiO2 (control) by factors of 3.5, 2.1, and 14.5, respectively. Linear regression of the ΔJ (current density difference) vs. scan rate plots (Fig. 6F) yielded slope (k) values of 69.02 (Ru-Fe-Ni/Ta2O5@DHB-GQD), 32.11 (Ta2O5@DHB-GQD), 16.24 (Ta2O5), and 4.77 (TiO2). Given that k correlates with ECSA for analogous nanomaterials [39], these results highlight three key insights:

Ta2O5's interconnected tunnels increase ECSA by 3.5× compared to TiO2, exposing abundant active sites. Smaller Ta2O5 nanocrystals formed with DHB-GQD elevate ECSA by 2× via enhanced surface area. Ru/Fe/Ni incorporation optimizes tunnel geometry, further boosting ECSA by 3.5× through improved active site accessibility.

Semiconductor properties and charge transfer dynamics of Ru-Fe-Ni/Ta2O5@DHB-GQD were systematically evaluated through UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectroscopy (DRS), electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS), and ion diffusion analysis (Fig. 7 ). UV–vis DRS (Fig. 7A) reveals distinct absorption profiles for Ta2O5, Ta2O5@DHB-GQD, and Ru-Fe-Ni/Ta2O5@DHB-GQD. Pristine Ta2O5 exhibits strong UV absorption (λ < 400 nm) but negligible visible light response, consistent with its wide bandgap (3.19 eV, Fig. 7B). Incorporating DHB-GQD extends absorption into the visible range (250–800 nm), reducing the bandgap to 1.36 eV via Ta5+ → Ta4+ self-doping. This reduction introduces mid-gap defect states, facilitating visible-light excitation. Further bandgap narrowing to 0.78 eV occurs with Ru-Fe-Ni incorporation, attributed to oxygen vacancy proliferation and metal-induced lattice strain, synergistically enhancing charge carrier generation and conductivity.

Nyquist plots (Fig. 7C) in 0.1 M KNO₃ quantify charge transfer resistance (R ct) using the equivalent circuit (inset). Ta2O5 exhibits the highest R ct (374.3 Ω), reflecting poor conductivity. DHB-GQD integration reduces R ct by 23× (15.6 Ω), while Ru-Fe-Ni doping achieves a 53× reduction (6.95 Ω), underscoring trimetallic synergy in enhancing interfacial charge transfer (Table S3).

Ion diffusion coefficients (D K +) derived from Warburg impedance analysis (Fig. 7D) increase from 3.9 × 10−12 cm2 s−1 (Ta2O5) to 6.1 × 10−9 cm2 s−1 (Ru-Fe-Ni/Ta2O5@DHB-GQD), a 1564× enhancement. This leap reflects the composite's hierarchical porosity and conductive graphene network, enabling rapid electrolyte permeation and ion transport.

The electrocatalytic performance of Ru-Fe-Ni/Ta2O5@DHB-GQD for urea synthesis was systematically evaluated under varying electrolyte compositions, potentials, and catalyst configurations. Linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) in Ar- or CO₂-saturated 0.1 M KNO₃/K₂SO₄ electrolytes (Fig. 8A) revealed distinct reduction behaviors. In Ar-saturated K₂SO₄, negligible current density (<0.5 mA cm−2) below −0.8 V confirmed the inactivity of SO₄2− and Ar at low potentials. A sharp current rise at −1.05 V corresponded to H₂ evolution, which exhibited high overpotential due to weak H+ adsorption on the catalyst surface. Substituting K₂SO₄ with KNO₃ significantly enhanced current density above −0.5 V, driven by NO₃− reduction. Similarly, CO₂ saturation further amplified current density, with CO₂ reduction outperforming NO₃− reduction, indicating preferential CO₂ activation. Notably, the combined CO₂/NO₃− system achieved the highest current density, with a distinct inflection near 0 V, suggesting CN bond formation—a critical step in urea synthesis.

Chronoamperometry (CA) at −0.5 to −0.9 V in CO₂-saturated KNO₃ (Figs. 8B, C) identified −0.6 V as the optimal potential, yielding 73.86 mmol g−1 h−1 urea with 48.5 % Faradaic efficiency. Below −0.6 V, sluggish NO₃−/CO₂ reduction limited activity, while potentials exceeding −0.6 V favored parasitic H₂ evolution (via H+ reduction), suppressing urea formation. This trade-off underscores the importance of balancing reactant activation and side reaction suppression.

Ten consecutive CA cycles at −0.6 V (Figs. 8D, E) demonstrated exceptional stability, with <2 % activity loss and consistent urea yield (72.4 mmol g−1 h−1 after 10 cycles), and Fig. S5 shows the cycle did not cause a noticeable change in the SEM image and XRD pattern, verifying an excellent structural stability. As a result, high structural integrity of Ru-Fe-Ni/Ta2O5@DHB-GQD, attributed to strong metal-support interactions and graphene stabilization, mitigated catalyst detachment or deactivation. Selectivity analysis (Fig. S6) revealed 48.5 % Faradaic efficiency for urea, outperforming intermediates (CO: 18.2 %, NH₃: 12.1 %, NO₂−: 9.8 %) and H₂ (2.3 %), confirming the catalyst's ability to steer reactions toward urea rather than byproducts.

Comparative studies with mono−/bimetallic analogs (Fig. 8F) highlighted the necessity of Ru-Fe-Ni synergy. Single-metal catalysts (S₁: Fe, S₂: Ru, S₃: Ni) achieved modest yields (2.75–3.35 mmol g−1 h−1), limited by their inability to concurrently activate CO₂ and NO₃−. Bimetallic systems (D₁: RuNi, D₂: RuFe, D₃: FeNi) improved yields (15.2–22.4 mmol g−1 h−1), yet remained inferior to the trimetallic catalyst (73.86 mmol g−1 h−1). Ru’s dominance in NO₃− reduction [40], combined with Fe/Ni’s roles in CO₂ activation and intermediate stabilization, enabled efficient CN coupling. This synergy, coupled with DHB-GQD's defect-rich structure and enhanced conductivity, established Ru-Fe-Ni/Ta₂O₅@DHB-GQD as a benchmark electrocatalyst for urea synthesis.

The electronic structure and charge transfer dynamics of Ru-Fe-Ni/Ta2O5@DHB-GQD were elucidated through density functional theory (DFT) calculations, including density of states (DOS), partial density of states (PDOS), and differential charge density analyses (Fig. 9
, S7).

The total DOS of Ta2O5@DHB-GQD (Fig. 9A) spans from −20 eV to 2 eV, with distinct features: A sharp valence band peak (−18 to −16 eV) arises from hybridization of Ta 5d, O 2p, and C 2p orbitals (Fig. S8). A broad valence band (−8 eV to Fermi level) reflects Ta 5d–O 2p hybridization, followed by a sharp conduction band peak (1.25 eV). Deep troughs near the Fermi level (−16 to −8 eV) indicate a wide bandgap (semiconducting behaviour), consistent with experimental observations. Introducing Ru (Fig. 9B) enhances DOS near the Fermi level via Ru 4d orbital contributions, forming a continuous band from −7.5 eV to 1.25 eV. This reduces the effective bandgap, improving conductivity. Further incorporation of Fe and Ni (Figs. 9C, D) amplifies this effect: Fe 3d and Ni 3d orbitals hybridize with Ta 5d and O 2p, eliminating the bandgap and creating a metallic-like DOS profile. This transition underpins the trimetallic system's superior conductivity and catalytic activity.

Differential charge density maps (Fig. S9) reveal electron accumulation (yellow) and depletion (blue) regions. Key observations: In Ta2O5@DHB-GQD, charge transfers from Ta (positive) to O (negative) due to O's higher electronegativity. Ru introduction (Table S4) redistributes charge, lowering Ta’s positive charge (0.915 → 0.607) while increasing O's negative charge. Ru retains a high positive charge (0.915), limiting its catalytic activity. Fe incorporation further reduces Ru’s charge (0.607 → 0.407) and Fe’s charge (0.116 → 0.042), enhancing electron density at active sites. Ni addition optimizes charge distribution, yielding Ru (0.407), Fe (0.042), and Ni (0.021) with near-neutral charges, facilitating reactant adsorption and CN coupling.

The trimetallic synergy induces: Metallic conductivity via Ru/Fe/Ni 3d–Ta 5d–O 2p hybridization. Reduced metal charges enhance d-orbital electron density, promoting CO₂/NO₃− activation. Charge redistribution alters TaO coordination, generating oxygen vacancies that stabilize reaction intermediates.

The reaction mechanism underlying urea synthesis on Ru-Fe-Ni/Ta2O5@DHB-GQD was deciphered through isotopic labeling and in-situ spectroscopic techniques. 1H and 13C nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) analyses were employed to trace the origins of nitrogen and carbon in the urea product. As shown in Fig. 10A, the 1H NMR spectrum of urea synthesized in 15N-labeled KNO₃ (K15NO₃) under CO₂ saturation aligns precisely with the reference 15N-urea spectrum, exhibiting characteristic doublets at 5.74 and 5.51 ppm. The absence of a peak at 5.63 ppm (corresponding to 14N-urea) confirms that nitrogen in urea originates exclusively from nitrate reduction. Similarly, 13C NMR (Fig. 10B) demonstrates that urea synthesized using 14CO2 matches the spectrum of 13C-labeled urea, verifying CO₂ as the sole carbon source. These results conclusively establish the dual-electroreduction pathway: NO3 − provides nitrogen, while CO2 supplies carbon for urea formation.

In-situ electrochemical FTIR spectroscopy at varying potentials (−0.5 to −0.9 V) revealed critical intermediates and bond formation steps (Figs. 10C–F): A peak at 2300 cm−1 corresponds to asymmetric CO2 stretching, confirming strong CO2 adsorption on the catalyst surface [37].The peaks at 1669 cm−1 (CO stretch) and 1434 cm−1 (CN stretch) emerge at −0.6 V, indicating co-activation of CO2 and NO3 −, followed by initial CN coupling [41,42]. A peak at 1460 cm−1 (O–C–O stretch) suggests the formation of a CO₂NO₂ intermediate, which undergoes hydrogenation to yield HOCONH₂ (3654 cm−1, OH stretch) [43]. Subsequent de-hydroxylation generates CONH₂, evidenced by NH bending (3329 cm−1) and rocking modes (1154 cm−1) [44]. Final CN coupling produces urea (H₂NCONH₂), characterized by amino group vibrations at 1638 cm−1 (bending) and 1154 cm−1 (rocking) [45].

The data collectively outline a stepwise mechanism. Electrostatic interactions between the catalyst's polar surface and reactants facilitate simultaneous adsorption. Ru sites drive NO₃− reduction to *NH₂, while Fe/Ni synergistically activate CO₂, forming a CO₂NO₂ intermediate. Sequential protonation convert CO2NO2 to HOCONH2, followed by OH elimination to yield CONH2. A second CN bond forms between CONH₂ and *NH2, completing urea synthesis. This pathway highlights the catalyst's ability to stabilize multiple intermediates and orchestrate sequential bond-forming steps, underpinning its exceptional selectivity (48.5 % Faradaic efficiency for urea).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Thermodynamic and mechanistic insights into urea synthesis
ce:section.ce:para:
The thermodynamic feasibility and mechanistic pathway of electrocatalytic urea synthesis on Ru-Fe-Ni/Ta2O5@DHB-GQD were systematically investigated through Gibbs free energy calculations and intermediate evolution analysis (Fig. 11
, S10–S24).

Density functional theory (DFT) calculations revealed strong adsorption of NO3 − (ΔG = −3.6 eV) and CO₂ (ΔG = −0.84 eV) on the catalyst surface, driven by electrostatic interactions with the polar Ta2O5 matrix and charge redistribution induced by Ru-Fe-Ni sites. In contrast, H+ adsorption exhibited weak binding (ΔG = −0.22 eV), explaining the suppressed hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) and enhanced selectivity for urea synthesis.

NO3 − undergoes a nine-step reduction to form *NH2 intermediates (Fig. 11A): Electrochemical Reduction: Sequential electron transfer steps convert *NO3 − → *NO2 → *NO2H → *NO → *NHO → *NHOH → *NH → *NH2. Proton-Coupled Electron Transfer: Critical steps (*NO2H → *NO and *NHOH → *NH) require overcoming minor energy barriers (0.46 eV), while others (e.g., *NO3 − → *NO2, ΔG = −1.2 eV) are thermodynamically spontaneous. Overall Energetics: The cumulative Gibbs free energy change (ΔG = −12 eV) confirms facile NO3 − reduction to *NH2, facilitated by the catalyst's dual-functionality in electron transfer and intermediate stabilization.

CO2 reduction proceeds via three steps (Fig. 11B): *CO2 → *COOH (ΔG = −0.9 eV), a spontaneous step driven by H+ adsorption. *COOH → *CO (ΔG = −0.84 eV), requiring a moderate energy barrier (1.98 eV). The total ΔG of −1.74 eV underscores efficient CO₂-to-CO conversion, critical for subsequent CN coupling.

The coupling of *NH₂ and *CO to form urea (Fig. 11C) involves: *NH₂ + *CO → *CONH2, overcoming a transition state barrier of 2.47 eV. Final Coupling: *CONH2 + *NH2 → *CONH2NH2 (urea), a highly exothermic step (ΔG = −1.67 eV). Despite the intrinsic challenge of CN bond formation [46], the low energy barrier and strong exothermicity highlight the catalyst's ability to drive this rate-limiting step efficiently.

The catalytic performance of Ru-Fe-Ni/Ta2O5@DHB-GQD was benchmarked against previously reported urea synthesis catalysts (Table 1 ), demonstrating unparalleled efficiency and scalability. Key competitive advantages of our strategy are outlined below: The metal-graphene quantum dot coordination strategy enables uniform dispersion of Ru, Fe, and Ni single atoms on Ta2O5 via robust coordination bonds between DHB-GQD functional groups (carboxyl, imidazolyl) and metal ions. This approach achieves a metal loading of 5.6 wt%—significantly exceeding conventional single-atom catalysts (SACs, typically <2 wt%)—while maintaining atomic dispersion. The water-soluble nature of metal-DHB-GQD complexes and compatibility with industrial processes (solution coordination, spray-drying, thermal annealing) ensure scalability, cost-efficiency, and environmental sustainability. Furthermore, the modular design permits extension to other d/f-block metals, enabling broad applicability in SAC synthesis.

Firstly, high catalytic activity via structural engineering. Ta2O5's bronze-type shear structure, characterized by 3D interconnected tunnels (lattice parameters: a c = 43.99 Å, b = 3.89 Å, c = 6.21 Å), provides a 3.5× larger electrochemical surface area (ECSA) than TiO2. DHB-GQD integration further refines tunnel geometry, reducing Ta2O5 nanocrystal size and amplifying ECSA by 4×. Combined with Ru-Fe-Ni doping, the composite achieves a K+ diffusion coefficient of 6.1 × 10−9 cm2 s−1—1564× higher than pristine Ta2O5—enabling simultaneous surface and subsurface catalytic activity.

Secondly. defect-mediated conductivity enhancement. DHB-GQD induces Ta5+ → Ta4+ self-doping, generating oxygen vacancies that narrow the bandgap from 3.19 eV (Ta2O5) to 0.78 eV. Concurrently, Ru-Fe-Ni incorporation amplifies vacancy density, while graphene sheets facilitate interfacial electron transfer via Ta–O/N bonds. These effects collectively reduce charge transfer resistance (R ct) from 374.3 Ω (Ta2O5) to 6.95 Ω, achieving a 53 times conductivity improvement.

Thirdly, trimetallic synergy for multi-step catalysis. The synergistic interplay of Ru (NO₃− activation), Fe (intermediate stabilization), and Ni (electron transfer modulation) enables efficient CN coupling—a rate-limiting step in urea synthesis. Compared to mono−/bimetallic analogs, the trimetallic system increases urea yield by 22× (73.86 vs. 3.35 mmol g−1 h−1), demonstrating unparalleled functional diversity.

In summary, we present a breakthrough in scalable synthesis of high-performance oxide-supported SACs via a metal-graphene quantum dot coordination strategy. The Ru-Fe-Ni/Ta2O5@DHB-GQD catalyst achieves to high urea yield of 73.86 mmol g−1 h−1 urea yield with 48.5 % Faradaic efficiency, surpassing all reported catalysts. Ta2O5's shear structure and DHB-GQD-derived defects create a 3D conductive network with 6.1 × 10−9 cm2 s−1 K+ diffusion efficiency. Trimetallic synergy lowers the CN coupling energy barrier to 2.47 eV, enabling efficient multi-step catalysis. A 5.6 wt% metal loading with compatibility for roll-to-roll manufacturing. In addition, this work redefines the design paradigm for SACs, offering a universal platform for applications in sustainable nitrogen fixation, CO₂ valorization, and energy-efficient chemical synthesis.

Li Ruiyi: Investigation, Data curation. Zhang Qingqing: Investigation. Li Xiaosuan: Investigation. Li Nana: Investigation. Liu Xiaohao: Software, Investigation. Li Zaijun: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Investigation, Conceptualization.
